artifacts.title: The deck IS an emissary — packet structure + manifest
artifacts.summary: Concrete packet spec for shipping the deck as an emissary: three-layer structure, a full YAML manifest, a Thomas cover note, and implementation notes.
artifacts.why: Operationalizes the self-referential pattern for the actual BMW/Hitachi send, providing copy-paste manifest and cover language so the deck demonstrates the product on first contact.

artifacts.title: Documents vs. Emissaries — rhetoric
artifacts.summary: A reusable comparison table and rhetorical triplet contrasting documents, prompts, memory, and emissaries by what each carries and how each fails.
artifacts.why: Gives the deck a tight, quotable way to differentiate emissaries from primitives investors already know, anchoring the solution section without abstract definitions.

artifacts.title: Self-referential one-pager pattern
artifacts.summary: A meta-design principle stating the seed deck should itself perform as an emissary, including an embedded YAML manifest and a recommended one-pager flow.
artifacts.why: Sets the architectural rule that makes the entire deck a working demo, pre-qualifying investors who get the meta-move while keeping the surface readable for those who don't.

artifacts.title: Three-tempo architecture — membrane/body/metabolism
artifacts.summary: Reframes the three-layer model as membrane/body/metabolism, defines the emissary atomic unit with four NOT-contrasts, and names continuity of agency across boundaries as the thesis.
artifacts.why: Supplies the cover-slide hero framing and the elevator pitch, while foreclosing the why-not-three-companies objection by making integration structural rather than a roadmap stitch.

artifacts.title: v0 — Deck Blueprint (14 slides + appendix)
artifacts.summary: The v0 14-slide deck blueprint with sequence, hero copy for slides 1-6 and 10, design rules, appendix structure, and links to per-slide artifacts.
artifacts.why: This is the master map for the BMW/Hitachi review version — every other seed-deck artifact slots into a numbered slide here, making it the spine the deck assembles against.

artifacts.title: Continuity bottleneck — generation solved, continuity not
artifacts.summary: Slide 3 framing that the AI-era bottleneck has shifted from generation to continuity, with supporting copy, visual options, and outside-the-deck reuse cases.
artifacts.why: Carries the why-now argument and separates Egregore from the crowded AI productivity category by reframing the problem investors think they understand.

artifacts.title: Handoff failure modes — what flat docs lose
artifacts.summary: Enumerates what flat document handoffs lose (intent, dependencies, judgment, next steps) and frames the repair cost receivers absorb to reconstruct missing context.
artifacts.why: Reference material for the problem section — gives the deck the wound it needs to make vivid, plus visualization options (iceberg, reconstruction loop) and an opening line.

artifacts.title: Hero line — AI work creates intelligence. Orgs lose it before it compounds.
artifacts.summary: Locks in the slide 2 hero line 'AI work creates intelligence. Organizations lose it before it compounds.' with supporting copy and three stamp cards.
artifacts.why: This is the chosen plain-language problem statement that replaces 'cognitive entropy' — the quotable opener that has to survive forwarding and land on first read.

artifacts.title: What-systems vs Why-system — split-table framing
artifacts.summary: Slide 4 split-table diagnosis contrasting what-systems (tickets, PRs, docs, chats, wikis) with the why-layer Egregore captures (rationale, decisions, tradeoffs, open threads).
artifacts.why: Positions Egregore as additive rather than replacement for existing tools, lowering enterprise threat profile and setting up the missing-primitive slide that follows.

artifacts.title: Five-slide problem sequence — each stat maps to an Egregore feature
artifacts.summary: Research-recommended 5-slide problem sequence where each stat maps 1:1 to a feature — $9M workslop → emissaries, +91% review time → egregore, 95% pilot failure → OCL, Yan fragile systems → emissary substrate, 275 interruptions → integration layer.
artifacts.why: Converts the deck's problem section from narrative to data-driven argument architecture where every claim is citable and every problem points to a specific Egregore feature.

artifacts.title: The Pretense Layer — sharpened problem framing
artifacts.summary: Framing B from the cognitive-entropy research — 'AI wrote it. AI will read it. The human in the middle pays the tax.' — a sharper problem framing that names the specific mechanism behind context loss.
artifacts.why: The single deck-defining framing the research recommends leading with — names a mechanism no competitor (Glean, Notion, Letta) has claimed, gives the problem a viscerally true one-liner, and points directly at emissaries as the cure.

artifacts.title: Agentic continuity — definition
artifacts.summary: Definitional paragraph for 'agentic continuity' — the preservation of task-relevant state across people, agents, sessions, and environments.
artifacts.why: Coins the deck's core term, bridging the problem-to-solution transition and giving investors a category label to remember Egregore by.

artifacts.title: Anatomy of an Emissary — 7-part cell metaphor
artifacts.summary: Seven-part cell-metaphor anatomy of an emissary (membrane, nucleus, organelles, etc.) with operational labels mapping biological parts to product functions.
artifacts.why: Slide 7 visual that finally makes 'centaur object' click for investors — surface for humans, interior for agents — using memorable, professional imagery.

artifacts.title: Context origin story — where context comes from
artifacts.summary: Architecture answer to 'where does the context come from?' — context is born in user agentic runtimes and written to a shared FS+KG via emissary exchange.
artifacts.why: Answers the first investor objection (vs. ChatGPT memory, wikis, RAG, observability) by showing context is generated at moment of work, not retrofitted.

artifacts.title: Cross-harness capture matrix
artifacts.summary: Matrix specifying how session evidence is captured per harness via filesystem-native or MCP paths, with a capture_capabilities metadata block recording per-bundle fidelity tiers.
artifacts.why: Proves cross-harness learning (not just cross-harness running) for the competitive and enterprise GTM slides and converts the data-we-have claim into an operational fact.

artifacts.title: Dashboard — five views
artifacts.summary: Five-view dashboard spec (ledger, context graph, egregores, permissions, distillations) that visualizes accumulating user value and natural upgrade surfaces.
artifacts.why: Makes the three-layer model operational for investors who ask 'what does the user see?' and shows monetization as natural growth, not paywall.

artifacts.title: The data social contract — 5 promises + transformation ladder
artifacts.summary: Egregore's product-level data posture: five day-one promises, an L0-L4 transformation ladder, and six ordering aphorisms framing data as situated contribution rather than fuel.
artifacts.why: Carries the enterprise GTM and competitive slides by making procurement, GDPR/CCPA/EU AI Act conformance, and the data-anxiety objection structural rather than a feature backlog.

artifacts.title: ECHO paper as OCL mechanism — action-consequence supervision
artifacts.summary: Maps the ECHO paper's action-conditioned prediction-error loss onto Organizational Continual Learning via a four-component action-consequence ledger, prediction, reward, and reflection stack.
artifacts.why: Gives the OCL/distillation slide a cited technical mechanism with benchmarks, replacing the soft-center promissory note that sharp investors would otherwise probe.

artifacts.title: What an Emissary Carries — four-layer anatomy
artifacts.summary: Slide-ready prose describing the emissary's four-layer anatomy (Intent, Context, Provenance, Instructions) as the minimum viable structure for continued agency.
artifacts.why: Anchors the product-anatomy slide by giving investors a compact, defensible answer to 'what is an emissary?' — structural, not informational.

artifacts.title: Emissary package architecture — 5 modules
artifacts.summary: Operational five-module architecture of an emissary package (frontend, frontmatter, MD body, embedded JSON, receiver hints) on top of Agent Skill conventions.
artifacts.why: Appendix A1 material that proves to technical advisors the protocol is real, host-aware, and sits on existing rails — neutralizing platform-risk objections.

artifacts.title: Eval corpus architecture — three-layer separation

artifacts.why: Turns the OCL distillation slide from a promissory claim into a shippable architecture and supplies the rubric-as-moat answer to the evals-as-defensibility objection.

artifacts.title: Four first emissaries — Bootstrap/Invitation/Harvest/Project Workflow

artifacts.title: Mini-app discipline — askuserquestions first

open_questions.text: Do we have any actual numbers we can quote at the seed stage?
open_questions.context: Even small-sample numbers ("emissary-equipped agents completed 73% more handoffs in our N=20 test") are better than zero. Worth a focused measurement sprint before the deck ships.

open_questions.text: Should we open-source the eval framework?
open_questions.context: Pros: research credibility, community contribution, faster trust-building. Cons: lower defensibility on the eval-set advantage. Probably yes, with the *eval set itself* (the data) staying proprietary while the framework is open. Defer to research-track strategy.

open_questions.text: "Continual improvement pipelines" is the phrase Cem used in his initial competitive answer.
open_questions.context: It's good but could be sharper. "Self-improving evaluation loops"? "Closed-loop evals"? Defer to copy review.

open_questions.text: Should we publish these invariants publicly?
open_questions.context: Pros: establishes category leadership, helps standardize. Cons: makes copying easier. Default: yes, publish (the moat is the network/data, not the protocol). Defer to product launch.

open_questions.text: Invariant 4 ("always ask first") creates friction for power-users.
open_questions.context: The non-interactive policy is the escape hatch but still needs to be opt-in per emissary. Tune the friction in v0.x based on real usage.

open_questions.text: Should there be an invariant about *versioning*?
open_questions.context: E.g., "Every emissary declares its schema version, and receivers refuse unknown major versions." Probably yes — adding this as the unstated 7th invariant when the schema policy is locked.

open_questions.text: Single-pick vs multi-select. Real sessions fail along multiple axes — an artifact can be both underspecified and stylistically off.
open_questions.context: Force single-pick (cleaner aggregation) or allow multi-select (more honest)? Lean: single-pick "main outcome" + an optional second-cause label.

open_questions.text: Who labels? Donor labels at completion is the default.
open_questions.context: But the donor is the sender or the receiver, not a neutral judge. Sender- labeled outcomes may over-attribute failure to receiver behavior; receiver-labeled outcomes may over-attribute to artifact/spec issues. Eventually need cofounder cross-labeling on a subset for inter-rater agreement.

open_questions.text: Calibration drift. "Mostly worked" means different things to different donors and to the same donor at different times.
open_questions.context: Does the taxonomy need anchor examples in the consent flow? Or is the noise acceptable once we have enough volume?

open_questions.text: When does outcome distribution itself become a metric? If `worked as intended` is 60% in month 1 and 70% in month 3, is that product improvement or donor-pool drift?
open_questions.context: Need to control for the rejection-funnel metrics before reading outcome rates as product-level progress.

open_questions.text: Public release. The internal outcome taxonomy is candid — "failed due to receiver behavior" is a strong claim.
open_questions.context: For the public whitepaper-companion subset of the corpus, do we publish raw outcome labels, or aggregate them? Honesty vs. agent-reputation tension.

open_questions.text: Does the taxonomy survive non-build emissaries? The seven categories are intuitive for build tasks.
open_questions.context: Are they still load-bearing for research emissaries, brief emissaries, dialogue emissaries? Likely yes — but worth pressure-testing against a few research-task sessions before locking the schema.

open_questions.text: External user donation: (post v0): the consent flow above is designed for internal-three-cofounder use.
open_questions.context: Extending to public donors needs ToS-level opt-in, configurable anonymization, and per-user visibility controls. Sketched in v1 plan; spec'd later.

open_questions.text: Revocation depth for already-published data: if a session has flipped to `public_release_approved` and appears in a whitepaper fork, revocation can tombstone the future but can't unpublish a paper.
open_questions.context: Need a clear donor-facing statement of this boundary before public release.

open_questions.text: Whether to publish the consent schema itself: pros — proves the governance is real, not marketing; cons — makes copying easier.
open_questions.context: Default: yes, publish, same logic as the invariants (moat is the corpus, not the schema).

open_questions.text: **How much of this lives in `egregore.json` vs.
open_questions.context: backend config**: default consent mode is per-user, but visibility staging policy might be per-org. Decide before multi-tenant deployment.

open_questions.text: Auto-donate is the riskiest default: even with the abort window, it changes the social contract.
open_questions.context: Probably ship v0 with `ask_each_time` as the install-time default, and let power-users flip to auto-donate explicitly. Re-evaluate after 30 sessions of real usage.

open_questions.text: Some lines (like "context layer for agentic organizations") could be the *title* of an essay or position paper, not just a deck line.
open_questions.context: Consider seeding a separate Cem essay around this phrase.

open_questions.text: Which lines are *most likely* to be misquoted or weakened?
open_questions.context: The "compound" verb in "AI work creates intelligence. Organizations lose it before it compounds" — easy to misquote as "compile" or "scale." Worth pressure-testing in pitches.

open_questions.text: Should we trademark anything?
open_questions.context: "Organizational Continual Learning" is generic enough to be unprotectable; "Egregore" is the company name; "Emissary" is generic. Defer.

open_questions.text: The biological metaphor (membrane / body / metabolism) is load-bearing for the three-layer slide but may collide with audiences who are allergic to organicist language.
open_questions.context: Worth A/B testing against the cleaner "interaction / context / learning" ladder in two parallel deck variants.

open_questions.text: "Sell contextual compounding, not data extraction" is a strong position-defining line but cuts off a future where we *do* sell cross-org learning (Phase 5 of the MVP roadmap).
open_questions.context: Either soften the line or commit to the no-cross-org-by-default posture as permanent — pick one before the deck ships.

open_questions.text: "The world is a loss function" is attributed to Papailiopoulos.
open_questions.context: Worth checking whether the line is original to him or earlier ML lineage (Sutton? Schmidhuber?) before printing it in any external surface. Attribution drift is the kind of mistake that costs credibility with ML-literate readers.

open_questions.text: "Continuity of agency across boundaries" overlaps semantically with "context layer for agentic organizations." Decide which is the *primary* north-star line and which is secondary — having two competing taglines weakens both.
Should we follow up with Thomas before sending the deck, or send the deck cold?
open_questions.context: Default: send the deck with the cover note (see `narrative/2026-05-23-cem-deck-as-emissary-packet.md`) and use the deck itself as the follow-up moment.

open_questions.text: Was anyone else in the room besides Thomas and the Hitachi counterpart?
open_questions.context: If junior associates were present, the deck needs to be parseable by them too — they'll be the ones writing the internal memo.

open_questions.text: Did Thomas signal a specific timeline for the formal review process?
open_questions.context: Capture in follow-up message if known.

open_questions.text: Should these be public-published commitments? Yes — see above.
open_questions.context: That is the source of credibility.

open_questions.text: Should the red lines be cryptographically signed? Probably not at launch.
open_questions.context: Adds friction without proportional credibility gain. Revisit if a competitor weaponizes "they could change it silently."

open_questions.text: Should the list be versioned and tracked in git? Yes.
open_questions.context: Transparency about evolution is itself a commitment.

open_questions.text: What is the process for adding new red lines? Open question.
open_questions.context: Probably a public proposal + 30-day comment window + commit, so that additions feel like governance, not marketing.

open_questions.text: What is the process for removing or weakening a red line? Almost never.
open_questions.context: If genuinely needed (a red line turned out to block a legitimate user need), it requires a public rationale, a deprecation window, and an honest "we changed our minds because X" post. Erosion-by-silence is the failure mode to avoid.

open_questions.text: Do we need an external auditor to verify compliance? Eventually, yes — at Phase 4 (hosted runtime) or Phase 5 (cross-org learning), third-party verification becomes a differentiator.
open_questions.context: Pre-Phase 4, the architectural design itself (transformation ladder, context boundaries, evidence linkage) is the verification — auditable by inspection of the artifacts themselves.

open_questions.text: Should we cite specific benchmarks in the deck, even placeholder?
open_questions.context: Investors love numbers; the lack of any number on the competitive slide reads as hand-waving. Need at least one usable number before the deck ships. Hold for now — see `_evidence/2026-05-23-cem-benchmarks-and-evals.md`.

open_questions.text: "Egregore compounds live work" is excellent but unfamiliar.
open_questions.context: Alternative: "Egregore captures work as it happens." Less rhetorically sharp but more immediately understandable. Both might work; A/B in pitch.

open_questions.text: Should this defense get its *own* slide in addition to the competitive slide?
open_questions.context: Probably not — would feel defensive. Better to make the positive case (3-axis frame) and trust that it implicitly answers the objection.

open_questions.text: "Strategic angels" — should we list categories or example profiles to signal what we want?
open_questions.context: Not in the deck (looks needy), but ready for verbal conversation. Example categories: founders of agent infrastructure companies, GTM leaders from horizontal SaaS, AI/ML research leads.

open_questions.text: The 15-month runway claim depends on the headcount plan.
open_questions.context: Confirm hiring plan against burn before sending.

open_questions.text: BMW Ventures and Hitachi Ventures are *both* corporate VCs.
open_questions.context: Do they prefer SAFEs or priced rounds? Some corporate VCs have mandate constraints (e.g., "we don't do SAFEs"). Worth checking before sending.

open_questions.text: Should the three axes be ordered?
open_questions.context: The current order (static→dynamic→portable→executable) reads naturally but doesn't have a defensibility hierarchy. Could re-order as: portable (foundational) → executable (productized) → dynamic (compounding) to suggest a layered argument.

open_questions.text: Specific companies in the appendix: which to name?
open_questions.context: Notion AI, Glean, Mem, Anthropic Memory, Linear Asks, Coda — all close enough to merit a mention but distant enough not to threaten. Hold pending appendix work.

open_questions.text: "Live work" in the hero line is a phrase that wants emphasis.
open_questions.context: Consider italicizing or otherwise visually distinguishing it. The contrast is with "the past" in the prior clause — make the contrast pop.

open_questions.text: Should we ask BMW/Hitachi which of the seven pilot surfaces is most resonant for their portfolio?
open_questions.context: It would be a useful diligence conversation that also self-qualifies them as strategic partners.

open_questions.text: The Figma analogy is good but somewhat tired in pitch decks.
open_questions.context: Is there a fresher analogy? GitHub-as-distribution-of-PRs is closer to our shape but less universally recognized. Linear-as-distribution-of-issues? Hold Figma for now; it works.

open_questions.text: "Bottom-up context creates top-down urgency" — should *urgency* be *demand* instead?
open_questions.context: Urgency feels truer but might over-promise. Pressure test in design review.

open_questions.text: Does "Pro Solo" need a different name?
open_questions.context: It's clear but flat. Some alternatives: "Pro," "Personal Pro," "Individual," "Solo." Default "Pro Solo" because it pairs nicely against "Team" and "Enterprise."

open_questions.text: Should there be a *Free Team* tier (small teams using the product free, with cap)?
open_questions.context: Pros: more virality. Cons: complicates the conversion story. Defer until usage data informs.

open_questions.text: Is OCL Distillation *separately* priced, or bundled into Enterprise?
open_questions.context: Probably bundled at first, then unbundled when the OCL feature set is rich enough to stand alone.

open_questions.text: Naming the category. Is "relational agentic artifact" the right category name?
open_questions.context: Or "agentic document"? Or "portable agentic packet"? Category-creation pitches need a name the investor can repeat. TBD.

open_questions.text: MCP-emissary integration story. Should we publish a position paper or spec showing how emissaries can ride on MCP transports?
open_questions.context: That would make the plumbing-vs-artifact relationship concrete and demonstrate non-rivalry rather than competition. Could open a partnership/distribution path with Anthropic.

open_questions.text: OpenAI Agent Builder coexistence. What does the integration surface look like?
open_questions.context: Can an Agent Builder workflow consume an emissary? Publish an emissary? Same question for LangGraph nodes. Worth a small experimental thread before fundraising conversations begin.

open_questions.text: Defensibility of the artifact layer. Protocols can be cloned.
open_questions.context: Artifact formats can be cloned too. The defensibility likely lives in the *substrate* (Egregore: identity, access, shared context, lineage) — not the format itself. That maps cleanly to the three-tempo architecture (emissary = membrane, egregore = body, distillation = metabolism). Worth a separate extraction on defensibility theory.

open_questions.text: Risk of being absorbed. If the artifact concept proves out, could OpenAI or Anthropic ship a competing artifact format inside their SDKs?
open_questions.context: Possibly. The bet is that artifact formats — like file formats, like HTML — tend to converge on open, vendor-neutral standards over time, and that the substrate layer (Egregore proper) is where durable value accumulates regardless of which artifact format wins.

open_questions.text: Does BMW Ventures actually have agents inspecting incoming materials?
open_questions.context: Probably not yet — but Hitachi might (or will soon). The agent layer is a bet on near-future behavior, not current behavior.

open_questions.text: If embedded manifest costs us *human* clarity (the PDF feels weird), we drop the embed and just attach a separate file.
open_questions.context: The human surface wins any conflict.

open_questions.text: Do we sign the manifest?
open_questions.context: Provenance is a sales feature — *this packet was signed by Egregore on date X* — but adds infrastructure cost. Defer.

open_questions.text: Does "Memory" as a primitive resonate with seed-stage investors, or does it read as too inside-baseball?
open_questions.context: Consider replacing with "Notes" or "Logs" for broader audiences.

open_questions.text: The four-row table is symmetric but `Memory → Traces → Recall → Noise/drift` is the weakest row.
open_questions.context: Sharpen or drop?

open_questions.text: Where exactly does the embedded block live?
open_questions.context: Footer of the title slide? Final slide? Sidebar on every slide? (Each has different tradeoffs.)

open_questions.text: Should personalized emissary blocks be generated per-investor?
open_questions.context: That's a product demo in itself but raises ops cost.

open_questions.text: Does the deck's *file format* matter?
open_questions.context: PDF flattens the executable layer; HTML/Notion preserve it; a hosted "emissary view" with parseable structure is the strongest demonstration but introduces a dependency.

open_questions.text: Does the metaphor scale across audiences? Membrane / body / metabolism reads beautifully for thoughtful investors and product people.
open_questions.context: Does it survive a corporate buyer audience (a Hitachi procurement officer) or does it sound too "lab-y"? Test in the next partner conversation. Possible mitigation: lead with buyer language in cold outreach; deploy the metaphor only when the conversation has earned the worldview.

open_questions.text: Should "membrane" be tested against alternates? "Vessel," "envoy," "ambassador" all carry adjacent connotations.
open_questions.context: Membrane wins because it implies selective permeability and is biologically consistent with body/metabolism — but if it tests poorly in cold reads, "envoy" is a near-synonym with stronger English-language resonance for "emissary."

open_questions.text: Is "metabolism" the right word for distillation? Alternatives: "digestion," "growth," "learning." Metabolism wins because it's ongoing, automatic, and produces the substance of the body itself — which is exactly the OCL claim.
open_questions.context: But "metabolism" is a less intuitive product word than the other two. May need a second-pass rephrase.

open_questions.text: Where does the four-NOT-contrasts list belong? Probably slide 4 (what is an emissary) or slide 5 (atomic unit).
open_questions.context: Not slide 1 — it's too defensive for a cover. The contrasts are clarifying weapons for Q&A and the FAQ appendix.

open_questions.text: How explicitly should the deck name "continuity of agency across boundaries" as the thesis? Strong argument for putting it on slide 1 as the central claim.
open_questions.context: Risk: it's a phrase that requires the rest of the deck to earn. If we lead with it, the deck must close the loop. Default: yes, lead with it; treat the rest of the deck as the proof.

open_questions.text: Is the appendix part of the same PDF or a separate "diligence pack" artifact?
open_questions.context: The "deck as emissary" framing suggests one packaged object.

open_questions.text: Slide 13 (competitive) and slide 11 (enterprise) both partially answer "how is this different from company brains" — needs careful sequencing so the answer doesn't get told twice in different framings.
Slide 14 bundles roadmap + raise.
open_questions.context: Splitting into two slides might be cleaner for a 15-slide version. Hold for now; bundle is tighter.

open_questions.text: The opening (slides 2–5) is 4 problem-side slides before introducing the wedge.
open_questions.context: Is that too long for an investor without you in the room? Could collapse to 2–3 with stronger sentences. Test before sending.

open_questions.text: Visual asset production is the bottleneck.
open_questions.context: The seven concrete visuals needed (mountain, three cards, chain, split-table, capsule, cell, flywheel) need to be sketched/designed.

open_questions.text: "Continuity" is doing a lot of work.
open_questions.context: Investors with a technical background may hear it as system-level continuity (uptime, state machines); product people may hear it as user-experience continuity (UX flow). Both are legitimate readings but neither is exactly what we mean. May need in-slide clarification: "Continuity of context — what enables one piece of work to continue another."

open_questions.text: "No longer generation" is a strong claim.
open_questions.context: If pressed, defend with: model costs falling, output per developer rising, but project velocity *not* rising proportionally — because coordination/context-rebuilding eats the gains. Have one data point ready.

open_questions.text: Is the audience "founders / operators" (who feel this acutely) or "VCs" (who may not feel it directly but recognize the pattern in their portfolio)?
open_questions.context: The framing tilts differently for each.

open_questions.text: Concrete examples land harder than the abstract list.
open_questions.context: Need 2–3 vivid scenarios — e.g., engineer onboarding, AI agent context-window churn, cross-team handoff in regulated industries.

open_questions.text: Should the cost be quantified ($X per engineer-onboarding, Y hours reclaimed per handoff)?
open_questions.context: Investors love numbers but soft costs are hard to defend. Hold pending evidence pass.

open_questions.text: "Intelligence" is the most ambiguous word in the sentence.
open_questions.context: Some readers hear it as "AI/AGI capability," others as "tacit knowledge / know-how." Test with three investors; consider "knowledge" as a swap if confusion appears. Holding "intelligence" for now because it stays plural with "Organizational Continual Learning" naming.

open_questions.text: The verb "lose" is passive — the org isn't actively destroying anything, it's just leaking.
open_questions.context: Consider "leaks" or "lets dissipate" as alternatives if "lose" feels accusatory.

open_questions.text: "Wikis" on the left is a small risk — some companies (Notion-heavy cultures) think of wikis as their why-system already.
open_questions.context: Could swap for "spreadsheets" or "comments" if testing reveals friction.

open_questions.text: Should the right column's items be *artifacts* (matching the left's "tickets / docs / PRs") or *qualities* (matching what we say is missing)?
open_questions.context: Current draft mixes both ("rationale" is a quality; "unresolved threads" is closer to an artifact). Acceptable for now.

open_questions.text: Is "agentic continuity" a term we want to own, or should we use it as scaffolding and replace with something more accessible (e.g., "transmissible work") for non-technical investors?
"Across people, agents, sessions, and environments" is four categories — does the deck slide list all four, or compress to two ("people and agents, across time")?
Is "membrane" the right word, or does "boundary" / "perimeter" read cleaner for enterprise?
open_questions.context: Test in design review.

open_questions.text: "Writeback channel" is technically accurate but sounds engineering-y.
open_questions.context: Alternatives: "feedback loop," "return path," "context exchange." Resolve before final design.

open_questions.text: The cell can show 7 parts or it can show 5 (collapse Provenance and Writeback into "lineage", collapse Agent manifest and Action interface into "agent surface").
open_questions.context: 5 is cleaner; 7 is more thorough. Probably render 5 in the deck and put 7 in the appendix.

open_questions.text: Need an actual illustration.
open_questions.context: Cannot ship this slide as bullet text.

open_questions.text: Should we name the technology under FS+KG (e.g., Git for FS, Neo4j for KG)?
open_questions.context: Pros: technical credibility. Cons: bakes in choices that may shift, and most investors don't care about the substrate. Default: leave unnamed in the main deck; name in A1 (appendix architecture).

open_questions.text: "Shared context runtime" is a phrase that needs sticking — say it on the slide, in the appendix, and in the manifest.
open_questions.context: Repetition will help it become category vocabulary.

open_questions.text: The 5-step origin story is too dense for slide 8.
open_questions.context: Either compress to 3 steps for the slide and put 5 in the appendix, or render as a sequence of cards/icons across slide 8 with one phrase per card.

open_questions.text: Are we already capturing from all five harnesses? Per the v0 spec, Claude Code and Codex CLI are the first targets; Claude Desktop, claude.ai web, and ChatGPT web are listed in the harness enum but the MCP `donate_session` tool may not be wired yet for the two web-chat hosts.
open_questions.context: If the deck claims five-host capture, the MCP tool needs to ship before the pitch — or we name the two web hosts as "supported targets" with a footnote, parallel to how the receiver-polymorphism artifact handles Antigravity.

open_questions.text: How do we explain the fidelity asymmetry on a slide? A naive reader could see "web chat captures less" and conclude "the web hosts are second-class." The framing has to be: *the corpus reflects the host's actual capability surface*.
open_questions.context: We don't fake parity we don't have. That's a feature, not a limitation — it's what lets eval results be trusted across hosts.

open_questions.text: Should the matrix name additional hosts: (Cursor, Aider, Antigravity, Pi)?
open_questions.context: Receiver polymorphism names Antigravity and Pi. Capture matrix currently doesn't, because the v0 capture spec only enumerated the five hosts above. Either we extend the matrix to match the four-host receiver table (and figure out capture for Pi / Antigravity), or we accept that *running* and *capturing* are different surface areas with different host lists. Probably the latter — capture follows where we have receivers shipped today.

open_questions.text: `capture_capabilities` schema versioning: this block will evolve (more capability flags as we ship more capture modes).
open_questions.context: Should it be versioned independently of `meta.yaml`? Probably yes — the eval layer will condition on it, and breaking changes to capability flags shouldn't require re-versioning every bundle. Defer the call to the emissary-evals quest.

open_questions.text: Hidden reasoning is never capturable today: — but Anthropic and OpenAI are both shipping limited reasoning visibility (extended thinking traces, o1-style reasoning summaries).
open_questions.context: If those become accessible via API or harness, the `hidden_reasoning: false` flag will need to flip to a richer enum. Don't bake "never" into the schema as if it's permanent; treat it as "not yet" with a versioned capability surface.

open_questions.text: Build order: which view ships first?
open_questions.context: Probably ledger (table view of emissaries) since it's the lowest-effort and the most-asked-for. Context graph requires KG infrastructure and a renderer. Distillations require OCL pipeline — last to ship.

open_questions.text: Do egregore views have their own dashboard, or is it the same dashboard scoped to the shared context?
open_questions.context: Decision pending — affects how multi-tenancy is architected.

open_questions.text: Mockups needed for the deck.
open_questions.context: Even sketchy ones beat zero. Designate one view (probably context graph) for hero mockup; others as small thumbnails.

open_questions.text: Where on the action-significance gradient does the ledger begin? Logging every Slack message is surveillance.
open_questions.context: Logging only formal decisions misses the dense middle. The pilot scope question is "what counts as a consequential action" and the answer probably differs per workflow.

open_questions.text: How is "predicted consequence" actually elicited? Free-text prediction is easy but low signal.
open_questions.context: Structured prediction (specific fields, ranges, probabilities) is high signal but high friction. The prediction-layer UX is genuinely unsolved.

open_questions.text: What is the update target? In ECHO it's a neural net's weights.
open_questions.context: Organizationally it could be: graph priors, agent prompts, playbook rewrites, human-readable lessons, decision-tree policies, or some combination. The "model" being updated is a composite object and we should be honest about that.

open_questions.text: What is the smallest credible pilot we can show to investors? One workflow, one team, one quarter of action-consequence pairs with prediction → actual deltas and a surprise register.
open_questions.context: Is that enough to validate the loop empirically before the seed close, or is it work for the post-seed roadmap?

open_questions.text: How do we make the "world model for free" framing honest without losing its punch? The framing is true at the algorithmic margin and false at the instrumentation margin.
open_questions.context: The cleanest version is probably: "free *given the substrate*. The substrate is what Egregore builds."

open_questions.text: What's the right relationship between OCL output and human governance? Auto-applied updates are dangerous.
open_questions.context: Human-reviewed updates are slow. The middle path (proposed updates with review cadence) is probably right but needs a concrete UX.

open_questions.text: Should "Instructions" be renamed to "Continuation" to avoid the prompt connotation?
open_questions.context: Investors may hear "instructions" and think LLM prompts.

open_questions.text: The four layers map cleanly to the agentic continuity question stack ("where are we / what matters / what changed / what next") — worth showing on a follow-up slide?
Should this file's table appear in the main deck or only in appendix?
open_questions.context: Default: appendix A1 — main slide stays visual. But have it ready if an investor pulls on the engineering thread.

open_questions.text: The `emissary_version: 0.2.0` in the frontmatter implies versioning discipline.
open_questions.context: We need a real versioning policy (SemVer for the schema; separate version field for individual emissaries). Defer.

open_questions.text: Token cost of embedded JSON is a real concern at scale.
open_questions.context: Mitigation path mentioned in source: short embedded manifest pointing to `payload.json` for large emissaries. Worth flagging on the technical roadmap.

open_questions.text: The "Schemas/scripts/references" trio is genuinely optional, but if we ship them with reference templates, adoption gets much easier.
open_questions.context: Decision: bundle a starter `validate-payload.ts` and `render-frontend.ts` with v0.2 so the protocol "comes with batteries."

open_questions.text: When does layer 2 start? ChatGPT's recommendation is "~30+ sessions per eval before authoring case cards." Is that the right threshold for our v0 internal corpus, or do we author one or two case cards earlier just to validate the pipeline end-to-end?
Who authors rubrics in v1, when external orgs join? Cofounder curation works for internal v0.
open_questions.context: For organizations using Egregore, does the org's own admin own rubric authorship for their slice of the corpus? Federated curation?

open_questions.text: Public-subset whitepaper fork mechanics. The repo is private.
open_questions.context: Some case cards will be selected for publication. Does the `publication_candidate` split live in the same repo with a visibility flag, or does a separate public repo fork on a different cadence? See `_evidence/2026-05-23-cem-governance-and-consent-design.md`.

open_questions.text: Rubric versioning across replays. If we edit a rubric mid-year, are previous replay results still comparable?
open_questions.context: Probably we version-pin rubrics per run, but the deck might need to say so.

open_questions.text: Is "case card" the right name for the deck? "Eval case" is accurate but dry.
open_questions.context: "Case card" is friendlier. Pick one and stay consistent across Slide 9 + Appendix A5.

open_questions.text: Does layer 3 belong on a separate slide? Currently folded into the OCL three-layer figure.
open_questions.context: If diligence partners care a lot about measurement methodology, the replay layer might warrant its own slide.

open_questions.text: Which Egregore does the Invitation Emissary point to at launch? Curve Labs' own Egregore?
open_questions.context: A demo Egregore? Both, with the visitor choosing? This matters because the Invitation emissary is the funnel from public to private — its destination shapes the first cohort.

open_questions.text: Does the Bootstrap Emissary install a Claude Code skill, or does it work agent-agnostic? Probably both, with the skill install being the optimized path for Claude Code users.
open_questions.context: Need to confirm what other receiver runtimes get on day one.

open_questions.text: What is the canonical content of each of the four emissaries? We should author them now, as part of the launch — not as hypothetical demos but as the actual emissaries the deck/site/ outreach links to.

Does the Harvest Emissary ship as one skill or as a small family? The data strategy lists harvest manifests, question intent, blind/ disclosed/comparative modes, evidence links — that could be a single configurable skill or a small bundle.

How do we narrate the Project Workflow Emissary in the deck without making it look like we are building a workflow tool? The framing must stay artifact-first: this is not "another LangGraph." It is a set of composed letters that happen to form a process.
Should the orientation question be customizable per emissary, or always the same four options (Run/Preview/Edit/Cancel)?
open_questions.context: Default: same four options for v0 (predictability), customizable in v1.

open_questions.text: The framed-TUI rendering of this is Pi-friendly but Claude Code doesn't have a native frame primitive.
open_questions.context: We may need to ship a small TUI helper for Claude Code that renders the orientation gate as a framed prompt. Tangent — defer to engineering quest.

open_questions.text: "Mini-app" is a phrase that should appear *somewhere* in the deck.
open_questions.context: It captures the centaur-object idea better than "context object" for some audiences. Test in pitch.

open_questions.text: Phase 0 → Phase 1 transition mechanics. The prior roadmap's continuum claim (accumulated context ports into the shared egregore on consent) is the right user-facing framing, but the per-phase data forms differ enough that the *port mechanism* needs its own design pass.
open_questions.context: Does a Phase 0 emissary "upgrade" by gaining context_boundary metadata, or does it get re-published with new metadata into the org's egregore? This affects the lineage graph.

open_questions.text: Phase 2 disclosure mode default. The harvest skill ships with blind / disclosed / comparative modes.
open_questions.context: Which is the safe default? Disclosed is the most respectful but reduces honest-answer rates; blind is the most epistemically productive but the most ethically fragile. Default likely depends on harvest type — needs a decision matrix.

open_questions.text: Phase 3 dashboard pricing. Distillation artifacts are the first layer where retention itself becomes the moat.
open_questions.context: Pricing should reflect this (the longer you accumulate, the more valuable each distillation), but we have not yet decided whether to price on retention duration, distillation frequency, or org size. Probably some combination; needs its own pricing extraction.

open_questions.text: Phase 4 readiness signal. When do we know an org is ready for hosted runtime?
open_questions.context: Some signal of trust-level + usage-depth needs to exist. This is also a sales-motion question — Phase 4 likely requires an enterprise design partner program before being publicly available.

open_questions.text: Phase 5 governance structure. Cross-org learning requires a consent + governance structure we have not yet sketched.
open_questions.context: Is this a customer council? An opt-in cooperative? A formal data trust? The legitimacy of Phase 5 depends on getting this right, and the right answer probably influences our incorporation form.

open_questions.text: Should the flywheel show explicit feedback arrows from Distillation back into Interaction (i.e., distilled playbooks improve new emissaries)?

open_questions.text: Is there a "Layer 0" worth naming?

open_questions.text: Should the slide name advisors / angels we already have soft commits from?

open_questions.text: Should Curve Labs be presented as a separate entity ("incubated at Curve Labs") or as our research alter-ego?

open_questions.text: The build-velocity numbers — should they go on the slide?

quotes.text: We are building the context layer for agentic organizations, starting with Emissaries.
AI work creates intelligence. Organizations lose it before it compounds.
The bottleneck is no longer generation. It is continuity.
Most systems store what happened. Teams need the why behind the work.
The missing primitive is portable context.
An Emissary is a portable context object.
Every Emissary carries the state needed to continue work.
When emissaries recur, they become a shared context runtime.
Context becomes more valuable the longer it lives.
Emissaries make distribution part of the product.
The artifact spreads first. The workspace follows.
Bottom-up context creates top-down urgency.
Figma spread through collaborative artifacts. Egregore spreads through agentic context artifacts.
We monetize persistence, depth, governance, integrations, and distillation.
Receiving should be free or nearly free. Persistent shared context is where monetization begins.
Receiving and limited participation should be free. Persistent shared context is sponsored by the egregore owner or team.
Company brains retrieve the past. Egregore compounds live work.

Company brains ingest what an organization has already written. Egregore captures why work is happening as it happens, makes that context portable through emissaries, and compounds it into an organizational learning substrate.
Dynamic, portable, executable context for agentic organizations.
Low-friction capture, human-visible provenance, permissioned sharing.
Continual learning does not mean uncontrolled model mutation. It means the organization's context, decisions, evals, playbooks, and agent behaviors improve over time under explicit governance.
Egregore reads the artifacts and interactions your agents already work with, then structures them into context that users can inspect, correct, permission, and port.
The context layer for agentic organizations.
Emissaries make work transmissible without making it flat.
An emissary is the atomic unit of the relational agentic web.
Continuity of agency across boundaries.
Emissaries are the membrane. Egregore is the body. Distillation is the metabolism.
We are building the relational artifact layer for the agentic web.
Agentic software in document form.
MCP is plumbing. A2A is plumbing. Emissaries are the artifact those protocols carry.
MCP is a protocol for tools. A2A is a protocol for agents. Emissaries are the artifact those protocols carry.

Not another runtime. Not another workflow builder. Not another chat memory. The missing object is a human-readable, agent-ingestible, URL-addressable, provenance-bearing, access-governable, forkable artifact.
Not another runtime. Not another workflow builder. Not another chat memory.
Plumbing standardizes how things move. Emissaries are what moves.
The protocols are racing to standardize the pipes. Nobody is standardizing what flows through them.
The recipient is the sensemaking boundary. Execution authority belongs to the runtime.
Legible before powerful.
Consented before harvested.
Bounded before intelligent.
Org-sovereign before cross-org.
Evidence-bound before distilled.
Exportable before sticky.
Data is not fuel. Data is situated contribution.
Inspectability before power.
Contribution, not capture.
Exit as dignity.
Egregore stores less raw exhaust than most AI companies, but stores much more structure, provenance, consent, lineage, and transformation metadata than most products do.
Store less raw exhaust than most AI companies. Store much more structure, provenance, consent, lineage, and transformation metadata than most products do.
Do not only learn from outcomes. Learn from the world's response.
An organization should not only evaluate whether actions succeeded. It should learn to predict what the world gives back after its actions.
Action → observed consequence → predicted consequence → prediction error → model update → better future action.
Surprise is not embarrassment; it is gradient.
Every consequential organizational act should become a supervised example for future action.
The world is a loss function.
Agency requires more than memory. It requires a model of the consequences of action.
The lock-in should not be "you cannot leave." It should be: your organization has accumulated a living, queryable, executable substrate that is too useful to abandon.
Sell contextual compounding, not data extraction.
Receiving should be free. Persistent shared context is where monetization begins.
Publish an AI workflow as a URL.
Send work to another person's agent without losing context.
Make your org's agentic work compound.
Turn team conversations into reusable organizational memory.
Run private agentic workflows with shared context and access control.
Agentic continuity is the preservation of task-relevant state across people, agents, sessions, and environments.
Agentic continuity: work can be resumed without rediscovering its context.
The point is not to maximize information. The point is to preserve the structure required for continued agency.
Documents transfer information. Emissaries transfer orientation.
Prompts trigger action. Emissaries preserve agency.
Memory stores traces. Emissaries make traces actionable.
Work doesn't fail because information was missing. It fails because the structure required to continue was missing.
High ambiguity, high repair cost, low continuity.
An emissary is a compression of agency.
Not a compression of data. Not a summary. Not a prompt. A compression of the conditions under which meaningful continuation becomes possible.
Effective continuity = task-relevant mutual information / context cost.
Communication quality = reduction in future coordination cost.
Effective communication is the transfer of enough latent structure for another node to act with low regret.
Flat documents transfer propositions. High-dimensional context transfers orientation. Adaptive communication transfers a living codebook.
The context is created at the moment of work, not reconstructed afterward.
Their corpus is finite and retrospective. Our context is continuous and generative.
The filesystem preserves artifacts and state. The knowledge graph structures people, projects, decisions, dependencies, and permissions.
Egregore is a three-tempo architecture of agency.
Emissaries are the relational transactions. Egregore is the persistent body. Distillation is the body learning from itself.
Egregore gives organizations a persistent body for their AI work. Emissaries are how intent crosses its membrane. Distillation is how the body learns from itself.
An emissary is an addressed, versioned, dependency-aware agentic artifact that carries intent, context, affordances, constraints, provenance, access rules, and a receiver contract.
Not a message. It persists and can be operated on.
Not a prompt. It has provenance, commission, output contract, surfaces, and lineage.
Not a workflow. It can be as small as a letter or as large as a dependency-aware process.
Not an app. The receiver's own harness can run it.
Every emissary's first runtime action is a bounded askuserquestions call.
When you receive an emissary, your agent opens it like an app: a bounded screen with options. Run, preview, edit, or cancel. Nothing happens before you choose.
We can ship agentic software in document form without becoming a full hosted agent runtime on day one.
We are the format. The format outlives any specific runtime.

Every major AI lab has shipped an agent harness and an agent protocol in the last 18 months. The portable artifact that moves between them — the unit of work an agent hands to another agent — has not.
Capabilities are portable. Tools are portable. Delegation is portable. Work is not.
The harnesses can run anything. The protocols can carry anything. There is no anything defined yet.
95% of enterprise generative-AI pilots fail to deliver measurable business impact.
quotes.attribution: MIT NANDA, The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025

quotes.text: 88% of organizations use AI in at least one function; only ~6% are high performers with >5% EBIT impact. Adoption is universal. Value capture isn't.
quotes.attribution: McKinsey, The State of AI: Global Survey 2025

quotes.text: The average knowledge worker uses ~30 apps per day and context-switches close to 3,000 times daily.
Employees using Microsoft 365 are interrupted every 2 minutes during core hours — ~275 interruptions per day.
60% of work time is spent on "work about work" — searching for info, switching apps, tracking down decisions, status updates.
28% of enterprises now use more than 10 different AI apps; 66% plan to raise their AI tool count over the next 12 months.
76% of enterprises have experienced at least one negative outcome from disconnected AI.
78% of AI users bring their own AI tools to work; 75% of global knowledge workers use gen AI.
Agent performance degrades by ~40% beyond 50k tokens due to attention dilution.
Long-horizon agent frameworks fail on >50% of samples — most failures due to hitting context-window limits.
Customer churn rises ~20% in support scenarios due to repeated re-explanations.
40%+ of agentic-AI projects will be cancelled by end of 2027 due to cost, unclear value, and inadequate controls.
AI inference costs dropped ~280x in 18 months.
The cost of compute is falling 280x while the cost of coordination is rising. The bottleneck has moved. Whoever ships the shared-context layer for human + agent teams collects the compounding.
Glean: $7.2B valuation, $200M+ ARR by Dec 2025, doubled in 9 months. Workday bought Sana for $1.1B cash. The category is real.
Glean is a destination; Egregore is a layer Glean could sit on top of.
We are looking for capital, design partners, and introductions to teams where context continuity across people and agents is already a painful operational problem.
Eight-plus unsolicited VC inbounds in three weeks. One formal corporate-VC pitch in motion. Zero outbound.
The deck has not shipped. The product has not launched. The signal is the request rate.
Three cofounders, three registers: vision, build, operate. Plus a research lineage that's been working on this for two years.
Curve Labs — two years of research into AI coordination, knowledge representation, and emergent ontologies. Egregore is what those years ship.
The deck the investor is reading was assembled by the product the deck describes.
We are already the first customer.
Predicting the next token well means you understand the underlying reality that produced that token.
The agent learns a terminal world model for free.
Organizational memory is not primarily semantic ("what do we know?"). It is dynamical ("what tends to happen when we act this way in this kind of situation?").
A launch email is not a communication artifact. It is an intervention.
The dense middle of organizational response is where most of the learning signal actually lives.
The instrumentation layer is exactly what Egregore's substrate provides.
A donated session bundle is not yet an eval case. It is raw provenance.
Do not mutate donated sessions to make them eval-friendly. Derive eval cases from them.
Anyone can collect sessions. The rubric layer is the asset.
Competitors can copy our raw sessions; they cannot copy the rubric without copying our judgment.
We have evidence, an instrument, and a measurement protocol.
Building a company brain is one project. Knowing whether it's working is a separate, harder project.
We measure baseline usefulness, handoff continuation, and distillation quality. Most agent infrastructure ships without any of these.
Today's customers are tomorrow's data moat — and the data moat is generated by usage, not by separate acquisition effort.
Accumulation is a continuum from the first interaction throughout the shared context lifecycle.

Emissaries will become their own runtime in the future so we can run emissaries in the cloud and have them regulate their respective stakeholders and contributions as truly living coordination artifacts.
Prove the artifact without becoming a data-processor monster.
Monetize better emissaries through shared organizational context.
Turn interaction into organizationally usable context — without becoming a profiling system.
Make accumulated context visibly compound.
Living coordination artifacts.
Proprietary intelligence without violating trust.
We make AI work compound across people, agents, and organizations.
Work needs an object that can travel: readable by humans, executable by agents, permissioned by default, and structured enough to continue from.
Human-readable. Agent-executable. Context-bearing.
Emissaries are the foundational primitive. Handoff is the first canonical use case.
One slide, one thought.
Every receiver experiences the value, and repeated interactions create a natural shared workspace.
Egregore is a substrate of organizational behaviour that any agent on any surface can read and write.
The corpus is a side-effect of work, not the input to it.
We have one corpus that spans the entire agentic ecosystem, with metadata recording exactly which evidence came from where.
The bet is on the category of agent harnesses, not on any single host.
Same emissary, different surfaces.
Every emissary is useful to the receiver before they become a customer.
Every item on procurement's checklist is baked into the protocol, not bolted on as an enterprise feature.
As AI data anxiety intensifies through 2026 and 2027, the products that win enterprise are the ones whose data posture is structurally credible.
The social contract IS the enterprise moat.
A quote is not a position. A position is an interpreted node supported by quote evidence.
The deck IS an emissary.
A deck that demonstrates the product through its own form does more persuasive work than a deck that describes the product.
Memory is per-user and per-product. Egregore is cross-user, cross-product, organization-wide.
RAG retrieves over existing docs. Egregore generates the context at the moment of work, then structures it into a queryable KG.
Observability captures logs. Egregore captures decisions and rationale — the why behind the logs.
We will not train global models on private org data by default.
We will not sell personal data.
We will not use private Egregore context to enrich another org.
We will not silently turn Harvest into performance review or personality scoring.
We will not store hidden fields unless the user explicitly chooses.
We will not make irreversible actions without human return points.
We will not make export and deletion a premium feature.
Credibility requires asymmetric cost. A private commitment costs nothing to break. A public commitment costs reputation.
Promises explain the intent. Red lines make the intent operational.
Consent is a structured, versioned, revocable object — not a checkbox.
Enterprise-safe isn't a SKU or a future roadmap item; it's the shape of the data model from day one.
Yes should not feel irreversible.
A corpus of only successful sessions teaches you nothing about failure modes. Failure cases are the training signal.
The difference between a product with metrics and a product with an instrument.
Multiple independent labs landed on the same primitive within 6 months.
The question is not whether it's right, but who will own the production version of it. Egregore's bet is that the production version is organizational, not single-model.
Continual learning ≠ uncontrolled model mutation.
An emissary can always be read before it is run.
Every emissary is a legible document first, an executable second.
The landing page is the product.
The system learns from its own use.
Generation got solved. Continuity didn't.
Where documents inform, emissaries orient. Where prompts instruct, emissaries situate. Where memory stores traces, emissaries compose those traces into actionable context.
Continue with situated judgment.

Egregore is an adaptive organizational harness. It observes human-agent work as it happens, structures that activity into durable context, detects recurring workflows and coordination failures, and gradually adapts the organization's tools, memory, emissaries, and rituals to fit the specific way that organization operates. The aim is organizational continual learning: not just preserving knowledge, but improving the organization's capacity to coordinate, remember, delegate, recover, and evolve.
Egregore is an adaptive organizational harness for human-agent work.
Observe. Structure. Detect. Adapt.
Coordinate. Remember. Delegate. Recover. Evolve. Compound.
Egregore observes human-agent work as it happens and adapts your tools, memory, emissaries, and rituals to fit how your organization actually operates.
AI wrote it. AI will read it. The human in the middle pays the tax.
The most expensive lie in modern work is the pretense that knowledge artifacts are still human-author-for-human-audience.
Workslop is the new shadow tax — $186/employee/month, $9M/year for a 10,000-person org.

quotes.text: Glean made enterprise knowledge findable. Notion made it collaboratively editable. Letta made an agent stateful. No one has made the institution stateful.
Cognitive entropy.
Atomized without bonds, atoms make heat — not molecules.
Capability needs a regulator.
